coredata.dc:title: Dynamic covalent carbon dot-based emulsifiers: A novel strategy for smart Pickering emulsions and enhanced heavy oil recovery in ultra-low permeability reservoirs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Junjie
Cui, Jianpeng
He, Tinglei
Zhang, Tianhao
Yuan, Xinyu
Hu, Jianwen
Gao, Haotian
Zhang, Hong
Li, Chunling
Hu, Songqing
Sun, Shuangqing
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), a class of fluorescent nanomaterials distinguished by their unique properties such as tunable surface chemistry and nanoscale dimensions, have garnered significant attention. In this study, we synthesize CDs-based emulsifiers through a combination of hydrothermal synthesis and hydrophobic modification utilizing dynamic covalent imine bonds, imparting them with amphiphilicity and pH-responsiveness. These carbon dot-based emulsifiers stabilize the oil–water interface to form Pickering emulsions and enable the reversible regulation of amphiphilicity through pH-induced cleavage and reformation of imine bonds, thereby facilitating transitions between emulsification and demulsification. Furthermore, the amphiphilic CDs effectively reduce oil–water interfacial tension and exhibit potential application in mobilizing heavy oil in ultra-low permeability reservoirs. Consequently, we observed a 28.78 % decrease in injection pressure and an 18.65 % increase in recovery rates. Experimental and theoretical results reveal that the CDs adsorb onto both solid and oil phase surfaces, modulating the interfacial properties and enabling the oil film attached to the rock wall to be more easily stripped off during water flushing. Additionally, the small size of the CDs allowed them to enter finer pore throats and effectively displace the residual oil film. This unique characteristic holds significant promise for future advancements in the fields of smart-response Pickering emulsions and enhanced oil recovery in ultra-low permeability reservoirs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dynamic covalent imine bonds
Pickering emulsion
pH-responsive
Enhanced oil recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by CDs-C
Characterization and analysis of CDs-C NPs
Factors of affecting Pickering emulsion stability.
pH-responsive behaviors and mechanism of the Pickering emulsion.
Interface activity of CDs-C NPs, microscopic displacement visualization test and core flooding experiments

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the oil displacement experiment.

(a) TEM image, (b) HR-TEM image and corresponding statistics of particle distribution of CDs; (c) AFM 2D images of CDs. (d) XRD pattern of CDs. (e) FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs-C; High-resolution XPS spectrum of (f) C 1s, (g) N 1s and (h) O 1s peaks from CDs; (i) Contact angle of CDs nanoparticles at different stages.

(a) Photograph, metallographic images, and (b) droplet size distributions of Pickering emulsions prepared at varying emulsifier concentrations. (c) Influence of emulsifier concentration on the creaming index and the rate of water separation.

(a) Photographs, metallographic images, and (b) droplet size distributions of Pickering emulsions prepared at varying water-to-oil ratios. (c) Influence of oil-to-water ratio on the creaming index and the rate of water separation.
(a) pH triggers the emulsion emulsification/demulsification cycle. Microscopic images (b, c) and mean diameter distribution (d, e) of the emulsion at the initial and after five cycles.

Morphology change (a, b, c) and mean diameter distribution (d, e, f) of CDs upon addition of NaOH and HCl, (g) Absorption spectra of CDs-C in various concentrations of alkali solution and (h) FT-IR spectra of CDs-C before and after alkali addition.
Schematic diagram illustrating the possible pH-responsive mechanism of the Pickering emulsion.

(a) Heavy oil–water interfacial tension (IFT) curves of CDs-C with various concentrations. Example Images: miniature model under different experimental conditions: (b) Initial conditions, (c) In brine and, (d) In brine with 500 ppm CDs-C, (e) Displacement experiment with 0.05 wt% concentration of CDs-C NPs and (f) Oil saturation distribution in the core at various stages of the displacement experiment.

head.ce:title: Dynamic covalent carbon dot-based emulsifiers: A novel strategy for smart Pickering emulsions and enhanced heavy oil recovery in ultra-low permeability reservoirs

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of Intelligent Energy Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), a class of fluorescent nanomaterials distinguished by their unique properties such as tunable surface chemistry and nanoscale dimensions, have garnered significant attention. In this study, we synthesize CDs-based emulsifiers through a combination of hydrothermal synthesis and hydrophobic modification utilizing dynamic covalent imine bonds, imparting them with amphiphilicity and pH-responsiveness. These carbon dot-based emulsifiers stabilize the oil–water interface to form Pickering emulsions and enable the reversible regulation of amphiphilicity through pH-induced cleavage and reformation of imine bonds, thereby facilitating transitions between emulsification and demulsification. Furthermore, the amphiphilic CDs effectively reduce oil–water interfacial tension and exhibit potential application in mobilizing heavy oil in ultra-low permeability reservoirs. Consequently, we observed a 28.78 % decrease in injection pressure and an 18.65 % increase in recovery rates. Experimental and theoretical results reveal that the CDs adsorb onto both solid and oil phase surfaces, modulating the interfacial properties and enabling the oil film attached to the rock wall to be more easily stripped off during water flushing. Additionally, the small size of the CDs allowed them to enter finer pore throats and effectively displace the residual oil film. This unique characteristic holds significant promise for future advancements in the fields of smart-response Pickering emulsions and enhanced oil recovery in ultra-low permeability reservoirs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Emulsions are omnipresent in numerous industrial processes and technological applications, playing a crucial role across sectors such as pharmaceuticals [1,2], cosmetics [3], food engineering [4,5], and materials science [6]. Achieving precise control over the stability and properties of emulsions is essential for optimizing performance and functionality in these sectors. While traditional surfactant-based emulsions have been extensively studied [7–9], their stability under harsh conditions and environmental compatibility remain significant challenges. Pickering emulsion, stabilized by solid particles, have garnered increased research attention due to their eco-friendliness and high stability [10,11]. Compared to conventional emulsions stabilized by organic surfactants, Pickering emulsions exhibit significantly enhanced resistance to coalescence. This is attributed to the irreversible adsorption of solid particles at the oil–water interfaces, which serve as robust mechanical barriers [12–15]. Furthermore, the use of solid particles as stabilizers offers additional advantages, including cost-effectiveness [1] and environmental sustainability [16]. However, traditional solid particles used to stabilize Pickering emulsions often lead to larger droplets sizes, hindering their application in fields such as drug delivery [17], catalysis [18], and enhanced oil recovery [19].

Carbon dots (CDs) possess unique properties, including biocompatibility, tunable surface chemistry, and exceptionally small size, which make them ideal green emulsifiers for stabilizing Pickering emulsions [20,21]. In recent years, CDs have found versatile applications in fields, including bioimaging [22], photocatalysis [23], supercapacitors [24], fluorescence imaging [25,26], and drug delivery [27]. The small size of the CDs, combined with their high surface area-to-volume ratio, results in increased adsorption at the oil–water interface, thereby enhancing stability and potentially improving control over droplet size, a critical parameter in various emulsion applications [28–30]. However, many practical applications, including oil recovery [31], emulsion polymerization [32], and multiphase catalysis [33], require rapid and efficient demulsification. This challenge can be addressed by using switchable or stimuli-responsive surfactants. These surfactants can reversibly interconvert between active and inactive forms under external stimuli, such as pH [34,35], CO2/N2 [36], light [37], redox [38], magnetism [39], and temperature [40]. Among these stimuli, pH has been extensively studied due to its simplicity and rapid response [41–43]. The imine-based Pickering emulsions demonstrate superior performance relative to conventional pH-responsive systems, owing to their distinctive dynamic covalent properties [44,45]. The reversible nature of imine bonds enables pH-triggered dissociation and reassociation while preserving excellent recyclability, thereby addressing the inherent limitation of irreversible breakdown in electrostatically stabilized emulsions [46]. Crucially, the covalent anchoring capability afforded by imine chemistry permits precise interfacial immobilization of functional components – a feature unattainable in physically adsorbed systems [47]. Moreover, the intrinsic modularity of imine linkages enables the construction of sophisticated multi-stimuli responsive platforms through orthogonal integration of additional triggers, representing a substantial advancement beyond conventional single pH-responsive emulsion systems. Unlike static covalent bonds, imine bonds can undergo reversible transformations in response to varying pH conditions. While dynamic covalent bonds (e.g., disulfide, boronic ester) broadly enable stimuli-responsive emulsions, imine bonds offer distinct advantages in mild-condition reversibility. Unlike disulfide bonds (requiring redox agents) [48] or boronic esters (sensitive to aqueous hydrolysis) [49], imine-based emulsions achieve pH-triggered destabilization without additives [50]. These combined advantages establish imine-stabilized emulsions as a versatile platform for advanced applications demanding both precise interfacial control and dynamic responsiveness. The dynamic yet robust interfacial architecture enables novel opportunities for engineering next-generation smart emulsion systems with precisely tailored properties.

Nanomaterials such as nano-SiO2, TiO2, and Al2O3 have been extensively studied for their role in driving heavy oil recovery [51–53]. However, the relatively large size of nanoparticles like SiO2 makes them prone to aggregation and plugging in ultra-low-permeability reservoirs, severely limiting their practical applications [19]. As the permeability of developed oil reservoirs decreases, the demand for smaller nanomaterials intensifies, necessitating the synthesis of nanoparticles with reduced dimensions. CDs are particularly suited for this purpose due to their high compatibility with micro- and nanoscale pores, stemming from their unique nanoscale properties. Consequently, CDs are poised to play a pivotal role in the exploitation of unconventional oil resources, such as tight oil and shale oil, both characterized by exceptionally low permeability.

In this study, a novel approach is presented to create pH-responsive Pickering emulsions using functionalized CDs as stabilizing agents. By incorporating dynamic imine bonds into the surface functionalization of the CDs, a system capable of reversible changes in amphiphilicity in response to pH variations has been developed. The dynamic nature of the imine bonds allows for a controlled response to pH changes, enabling efficient switching between stable and unstable emulsion states. To our knowledge, such a system utilizing CDs with this mechanism has not been previously reported. Furthermore, advanced microscopic techniques and spectroscopic analyses have been employed to elucidate the underlying mechanisms linking dynamic imine bond chemistry to the observed emulsion behavior. The findings provide crucial insights into the design principles of pH-responsive Pickering emulsions, paving the way for the development of more sophisticated and adaptable emulsion systems with broad potential applications. Additionally, nano-sized amphiphilic CDs significantly reduce the oil–water interfacial tension, offering substantial potential for application in ultra-low-permeability reservoirs to enhance crude oil recovery and provide technical support for addressing bottlenecks in low-permeability reservoir development.

Citric acid (CA) (99.5 %), ethylenediamine, (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES, 98 %), and liquid paraffin (99 %) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (99.8 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36–38 %), concentrated ammonia (NH3·H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR), and carbinol (99.5 %) were obtained from Sinopharm (Shanghai, China). Cinnamaldehyde (98 %) was purchased from MacLean (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout all experiments. Crude oil was obtained from Hekou Oil Production Plant of Shengli Oilfield in China, and a mixture of crude oil and kerosene (density: 0.82 g/cm3) was used as simulated formation oil. Cores with a permeability of 5 mD and a void volume of 15 % were purchased from Hai'an Petroleum Scientific Research Instrument Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

3.15 g of citric acid (CA) was dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol, followed by the addition of 1 mL of ethylenediamine. The resulting solution was transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined reaction vessel and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the brown-black, transparent product was dialyzed (molecular weight cutoff = 3500 Da) against deionized water for 12 h to isolate pure CDs
(2)
Preparation of Amino-functionalized CDs NPs (CDs-NH2)

0.1 mg of as-prepared CDs and 400 μL of ammonia (28 wt%) were added to 30 mL of anhydrous alcohol, respectively, and sonicated for 15 min to ensure homogeneity. Then, 200 μL of APTES was injected into the solution and sonicated for another 30 min. After that, the mixture was vigorously stirred on a vortex mixer for 3 h at room temperature. Finally, excess reactants were removed by centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 20 min) and APTES-coated CDs (CDs-NH2) were obtained
(3)
Modification of surfactants to form CDs-C

0.1 g of CDs-NH2 NPs and 108 μL of cinnamaldehyde were mixed in 30 mL of methanol and reacted under ultrasonic stirring for 6 h. The methods for cleaning, collection, and drying of the resulting CDs-C NPs were identical to those described in step (2)
The preparation process of emulsifier was shown in Fig. 1

The FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Optics, Germany) was utilized to analyze the functional groups present in the samples of CDs, CDs-NH2 and CDs-C NPs. This analysis provided insights into the molecular structures of the products. The element composition of CDs was obtained from a Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). A USB 2000+ spectrometer (Ocean Optics Inc., USA) was used for UV–vis spectrum of CDs. The morphologies of the CDs microspheres were investigated using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100plus, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The size/height distribution of particles was measured by atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon, German). The crystal structures of the CDs microspheres were determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD, BRUKER AXS GMBH D8-Focus) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å), at a voltage of 30 kV and a current of 30 mA. The wettability of the samples was measured using a contact angle measuring instrument (JC2000D2). Photos of the emulsion droplets stabilized by CDs were taken with a digital camera (Nikon, COOLPIX S620) and optical micrographs of the emulsion droplets on glass slides were recorded using a microscope (Nanjing Jiangnan Novel Optics Co. Ltd., China). The particle size distribution and average particle sizes of the CDs microspheres and emulsion droplets were calculated using Nano Measurer 1.2 software. The emulsion fractions were calculated by measuring the heights of the emulsion phases and those of the liquid phases. The oil saturation distribution of the core at various displacement stages was characterized by a low-field nuclear magnetic resonance displacement device (Suzhou Niumag Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by CDs-C
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of Pickering emulsions, CDs-C NPs were dispersed in 7 mL of deionized water via ultrasonication. An equal volume of liquid paraffin was then added to the dispersion, and the mixture was homogenized using an RE-2000B homogenizer. The concentrations of CDs-C NPs were expressed relative to the aqueous phase.

The droplet morphology of Pickering emulsions was examined by optical microscopy. Diluted emulsion samples were dropped onto a glass plate and observed under an optical microscope (ZEISS, Germany). The size of the emulsion droplets was counted using Image Pro Plus 6.0. The emulsion was poured into a measuring cylinder, and the total amount of emulsion as well as the precipitated oil and water at different time stages were recorded. The creaming index (CI) [54,55] and water separation rate (SR) [56] of the emulsion were calculated using the following formulas, respectively.
(1)
C I
( % )
=
H s
H t
× 100 %
(2)
S R
( % )
=
V
sw
V w
× 100 %
where Hs is the total height of the water plus oil phases, Ht is the emulsion height, Vsw is the volume of the precipitated water phase, and Vw is the volume of initially added deionized water.

The demulsification of Pickering emulsion can be achieved by adding an appropriate amount of 0.1 mol/L NaOH. Subsequently, the Pickering emulsion can be reformulated by adding 0.1 mol/L HCl and homogenizing for 4 min. This process can be repeated as needed.

The oil–water interfacial tensions (IFTs) were measured at 55°C using a Texas model 500 spinning drop tensiometer. Equipped with an image-capture device and image-acquisition software, this instrument can automatically measure and record the IFT values.

The procedure for the micromodel test was as follows: after being vacuumed, the micromodel was saturated with brine, and then the brine was displaced by crude oil until no more water was produced. Due to the higher viscosity of the crude oil compared to the resident water, almost all of the water present in the micromodel was displaced. After saturation with heavy oil, the model was aged for 24 h. Then, a brine solution was injected into the micromodel at a constant flow rate of 0.1 mL/min. Finally, 500 ppm CDs-C nanofluid was injected into the micromodel at a constant flow rate of 0.1 mL/min. Using a video recorder and camera apparatus, the microscopic flooding test could be visualized during the different stages of fluid injection.

First, cores were cleaned and dried at 60 °C for 48 h. Subsequently, the dried core was saturated with brine (4 wt% NaCl and 1 wt% CaCl2) under vacuum, and weighed to obtain the pore volume and porosity of the core. Simulated oil was injected into the core under vacuum until it was saturated and then aged at 60 °C for 7 days to simulate oil distribution. Following these measurements, core displacement experiments were conducted at 60 °C to mimic geological formation conditions. The procedure began by placing the saturated core in the core holder and applying 3 MPa of confining pressure. Initial experiments used saltwater to displace oil until no further oil production was observed. This procedure was repeated using CDs-C nanofluid, followed by subsequent brine flooding. The oil output volume from the saturated oil cores, pressure values and water cut were monitored in real-time until no more oil was produced (Fig. 2 ). The drag-reduction rate (P) was calculated using the formula (3).
(3)
P % =
P 1
-
P 2
÷
P 1
where P 1 is the pressure value after the initial water drive, and P 2 is the final pressure after the displacement process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and analysis of CDs-C NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image displays the size of the CDs ranging from 1 to 3 nm with an average diameter of approximately 2.1 nm (Fig. 3 a). As shows in Fig. 3b, the lattice spacing of 0.215 nm corresponds well with the spacing between graphene (100) layers. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was utilized to scan a 1 μm2 area of the mica surface coated with a low-concentration aqueous dispersion of CDs, and the 2D height distribution and microstructure of the particles were characterized. The AFM 2D images reveal excellent distribution and the absence of obvious agglomeration of CDs. The section height of the CDs ranges from 1.0 to 2.5 nm, with an average height of approximately 2.2 nm (Fig. 3c), which aligns well with the size distribution obtained by TEM analysis. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) profile of CDs presented in Fig. 3d exhibits a prominent broad peak at 22.4°, indicative of a graphite-like structure, in accordance with previously reported spectra of CDs [57]. FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to investigate the surface functional groups of the CDs and CDs-C NPs (Fig. 3e). The CDs curve shows a broad peak centered at ∼3500 cm−1 attributed to OH stretching vibration, while the low-intensity peaks at ∼1650 and ∼1050 cm−1 are assigned to CO stretching vibrations and CO bending vibrations. These characteristic absorption bands suggest the presence of numerous hydroxyl groups and carboxyl group on the CDs’ surface. However, distinct bands are observed for the CDs-C NPs. In the CDs-C curve, a broad peak centered at 3340 cm−1 is attributed to OH and NH stretching vibration, the peak centered at ∼1500 cm−1 and 880 cm−1 are assigned to benzene skeleton vibrations, and the peaks centered at ∼1070 cm−1 and 750 cm−1 are identified as CN stretching vibrations and bending vibrations, respectively.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed to analyze the surface chemical composition of the CDs (Fig. 3f–h). In the detailed XPS spectra, the C1s peaks (Fig. 3f) at 285.1 eV, 286 eV, 288.5 eV, and 291 eV are attributed to CC (sp2), CN/CO (sp3), CO/CN (sp2) and π–π* satellite, respectively. The presence of π–π* confirms the successful synthesis of CDs. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3g) resolves into two peaks at 399.87 and 400.32 eV, corresponding to –NH and CN, respectively. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3h) comprises two peaks centered at 531.3 and 532.2 eV, due to CO and COH, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra further reveal that the abundance of CO, CN, CO, and OH bonds in the CDs. This result is also supported by the FT-IR spectrum. Amphiphilic nanoparticles can effectively stabilize oil–water phases to form a stable Pickering emulsion (Fig. S1). The contact angles of CDs, CDs-NH2, and CDs-C NPs were measured through five replicate trials to calculate the average value, with a measurement error of less than ±3° for the water contact angle. As illustrated Fig. 3i, the CDs NPs exhibited the lowest contact angle, approximately 30°. After grafting APTES onto the CDs NPs’ surface, the short carbon chain of APTES slightly increased surface hydrophobicity, resulting in a contact angle of approximately 43°. In contrast, modification with cinnamaldehyde significantly increased the contact angle of CDs-C NPs to approximately 73°, indicating that the carbon chain of cinnamaldehyde substantially enhances the hydrophobicity of CDs-NH2 NPs. Since the density of imine groups plays a crucial role in pH responsiveness, precise determination of cinnamaldehyde chain surface density on the CDs NPs is essential. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) comparing unmodified and cinnamaldehyde-modified CDs NPs revealed a 6.9 % mass loss (Fig. S2), which we attribute to cinnamaldehyde decomposition. Based on this quantitative analysis, the surface density of cinnamaldehyde chains was determined to be approximately 6.9 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors of affecting Pickering emulsion stability.
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of Pickering emulsion is strongly influenced by both droplet size and nanoparticles concentration. To investigate this, the effect of CDs-C NPs concentration on emulsion stability was systematically studied (Fig. 4 a). At a nanoparticle concentration of 0.05 wt%, immediate phase separation was observed, leading to complete separation of the oil and water phases. This instability is attributed to an insufficient number of CDs-C NPs to effectively stabilize the emulsion and prevent droplet coalescence. However, stable Pickering emulsions were obtained at concentrations ranging from 0.1 wt% to 0.5 wt%, exhibiting no observable oil–water separation after one week of storage. As the NP concentration increased, the mean emulsion droplet diameter (Fig. 4b), water separation rate and creaming index (Fig. 4c) initially declined before reaching a constant level. This suggests an initial increase in emulsion stability followed by a stabilization effect. It can be hypothesized that at this stage, the surface coverage of the emulsion droplets increases progressively with the rising concentration of CDs-C NPs. The enhanced stability at higher concentrations is attributed to increased interfacial coverage by the CDs-C NPs, creating a densely packed particle layer that effectively prevents droplet coalescence[58]. The subsequent stabilization at higher concentrations is likely due to a balance between increased steric stabilization and the emergence of NPs aggregation at high particle densities, as previously reported[59].

To further investigate the effect of the oil-to-water volume ratio on the stability of emulsions stabilized by CDs-C NPs, liquid paraffin was chosen as the oil phase, and the oil-to-water volume ratio was varied from 4:1 to 1:3. The stability of the emulsion was evaluated by analyzing the emulsification index, the rate of water separation, and the microscopic droplet sizes. Fig. 5 b demonstrates a trend of decreasing average droplet size as the oil-to-water ratio decreased. This reduction in droplet size is due to the fixed number of stabilizing particles interacting with a progressively smaller oil volume, thereby increasing their concentration at the oil–water interface. The increased particle density facilitates the formation of smaller oil droplets. Conversely, the water separation rate and emulsification index exhibited a non-monotonic relationship with the oil-to-water ratio (Fig. 5c). Both parameters reached a minimum at an oil-to-water ratio of 1:1, indicating the highest emulsion stability. This optimal stability is attributed to the maximized particle packing density at the oil–water interface, leading to more effective steric stabilization. However, as the oil-to-water ratio decreased further (i.e., ratios of 1:2 and 1:3), the water separation rate and emulsification index increased, suggesting a decrease in emulsion stability. This instability is likely due to decreased particle coverage of the interface, and increased interparticle interactions leading to aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-responsive behaviors and mechanism of the Pickering emulsion.
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the pH-responsive cycling performance of the Pickering emulsion, HCl and NaOH were added to adjust the pH value of the emulsion, thereby controlling the emulsification and demulsification process (Fig. 6 a). Under neutral conditions, a stable Pickering emulsion is formed. When the pH is adjusted to 10.5 by adding NaOH, phase separation occurs within 3 min after gently shaking. Conversely, when the pH of the emulsion system is reduced to 5 by adding HCl, a stable emulsion is re-obtained after 4 min of homogenization. After repeating the emulsification and demulsification process five times using the same method, a stable Pickering emulsion can still be achieved. However, as the number of cycles increases, the droplet size of the Pickering emulsion slightly increases (Fig. 6b–e). This may be attributed to the accumulation of salt in the emulsion due to the addition of HCl and NaOH, which affects the stability of the Pickering emulsion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interface activity of CDs-C NPs, microscopic displacement visualization test and core flooding experiments
ce:section.ce:para: Reduced interfacial tension is critical for the formation and stability of Pickering emulsions. The surface properties and concentration of nanoparticle directly influence interfacial tension, thereby affecting emulsion stability. Additionally, interfacial tension is a key factor influencing heavy oil recovery. By decreasing the interfacial tension between crude oil and water, enhanced oil recovery can be achieved, enabling water to more effectively displace oil from reservoir pore spaces. Fig. 9 a illustrates a sharp decline in interfacial tension as the emulsifier concentration increases, especially at low concentrations (ranging from 0 % to 0.2 %). At higher emulsifier concentrations (between 0.3 % and 0.5 %), the rate of interfacial tension reduction flattens out, approaching a stable value. This demonstrates that the emulsifier effectively lowers oil–water interfacial tension within a certain concentration range, but beyond this point, the reduction in interfacial tension becomes less significant.

A glass micromodel was employed to simulate nanofluid and oil flow within reservoir pore spaces and evaluate displacement mechanisms. Fig. 9b–d displays a microscopic image of the oil–water distribution in the porous medium following the termination of the crude oil water drive. A 0.5 % NaCl solution, mimicking formation water, was used in the water-driven microscopic oil displacement experiments. Fig. 9 c reveals a focused water flow channel formed during the water flooding process, leaving substantial residual oil unaffected on the model’s diagonal sides, resulting in a low recovery rate during this water drive stage. Injection of a 500 ppm CDs aqueous solution into the micromodel (Fig. 9d) let to the formation of oil-in-water (O/W) or water-in-oil (W/O) emulsions within the oil-bearing region. This is primarily attributed to the surfactant properties of CDs-C NPs, which decrease the oil–water interfacial tension. Consequently, oil droplets detach more readily from the pore walls. Moreover, the passage of nanofluids through the micromodel significantly altered the pore wall wettability to water-wet, facilitating oil displacement from the walls and improving oil recovery.

Fig. 9e presents the results of the core displacement experiment, documenting the changes in oil recovery, water cut, and pressure throughout the injection of core pore volume (PV). Specifically, the oil displacement efficiency of nanofluids containing 500 ppm of CDs-C was investigated at 55 °C. The experiment was divided into three stages: initial water flooding, chemical flooding, and subsequent water flooding. It was observed that the stable pressure during initial water flooding was ∼1.2 MPa due to the consistent permeability and injection rate. However, the stable pressure during subsequent water flooding varied significantly after injecting different solutions. The CDs-C nanofluid exhibited good pressure reduction performance and exceptional oil displacement efficiency, with a pressure drop reduction rate of 27.78 % and an enhanced oil recovery of 18.65 %. This may be attributed to the CDs adsorbing onto both the solid and oil phase surfaces, modulating the interfacial properties and enabling the oil film attached to the rock wall to be more easily stripped off during water flushing. Additionally, the small size of the CDs size allowed them to enter the finer pore throats and effectively displace the residual oil film, thereby enhancing oil recovery and reducing injection pressure. Thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. S2) revealed that the CDs-C NPs exhibit outstanding thermal stability, maintaining >95 % of their initial mass at 80 °C under simulated reservoir conditions. This exceptional thermal resistance underscores their potential for practical deployment in reservoir environments.

To evaluate changes in oil–water distribution under different displacement methods, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) imaging was employed to characterize the oil saturation distribution within the core at various displacement stages. The results, as shown in Fig. 9f, indicate that in the saturated oil state, high-intensity signals (red and orange regions) were detected in the NMR images, suggesting that the core pores were nearly fully saturated with oil at the initial stage. This fully saturated state established the baseline conditions for subsequent displacement processes. After conventional water drive, a decrease in oil saturation was observed, as evidenced by the reduction of red regions and the gradual expansion of blue-violet regions. These changes suggest that oil in certain areas was displaced by water; however, due to the complex pore structure of low-permeability reservoirs, significant amounts of residual oil remained unrecovered. Following the injection of CDs nanofluid, the oil distribution within the core was significantly altered. Compared with the water drive stage, further reductions in red regions and notable expansions of blue-violet regions were observed, indicating a substantial decline in oil saturation. This result demonstrates that CDs nanofluid was effective in displacing residual oil that could not be reached by conventional water drive. During the subsequent water drive stage after microemulsion injection, further reductions in oil saturation were evident, although the magnitude of change was significantly smaller than that observed during the CDs nanofluid injection stage. This suggests that most of the residual oil had already been stripped and displaced by CDs nanofluid, with the subsequent water drive primarily serving as an auxiliary process to scavenge small amounts of remaining oil. Overall, CDs nanofluid demonstrates significant potential in ultra-low permeability reservoirs, as they can effectively enhance crude oil recovery and provide technical support for overcoming bottlenecks in low-permeability reservoir development.

In conclusion, we have successfully designed and synthesized a novel pH-responsive Pickering emulsion stabilized by functionalized carbon dots (CDs). These CDs, incorporating dynamic imine bonds, exhibit a reversible change in amphiphilicity in response to pH variations, thereby enabling controlled emulsification and demulsification processes. We conducted a thorough investigation into the formation, stability, and pH-responsive behavior of these emulsions, characterizing their properties under various pH conditions and elucidating the underlying mechanisms. The results indicate that both the emulsifier concentration and the oil–water ratio significantly influence the stability and droplet size of the oil–water emulsions. Notably, the emulsion stability exhibited pH-switchable behavior, which could be precisely controlled by adjusting the system's pH. Specifically, under alkaline conditions, phase separation occurred after gently shaking the emulsion for 3 min. Conversely, upon neutralization with HCl, a stable emulsion was re-established following 4 min of homogenization. These carbon dot-based emulsifiers stabilize the oil–water interface to form Pickering emulsions and enable the reversible regulation of amphiphilicity through the pH-induced cleavage and reformation of imine bonds. This mechanism facilitates a seamless transition between emulsification and demulsification. Remarkably, even after five consecutive cycles of emulsification and demulsification using the same method, stable Pickering emulsions could still be obtained, highlighting their excellent reusability. This study contributes to the advancement of stimuli-responsive emulsion systems and opens up new possibilities for various applications. Additionally, CDs-based nanofluids exhibit significant potential in ultra-low permeability reservoirs. The adsorption of CDs onto both solid and oil phase surfaces modulates the interfacial properties, facilitating the detachment of the oil film from the rock wall during water flushing. Furthermore, the small size of CDs allows them to enter finer pore throats, effectively displacing the residual oil film. This results in a notable 28.78 % reduction in injection pressure and an impressive 18.65 % increase in oil recovery, providing valuable technical support for overcoming the challenges associated with low-permeability reservoir development.

Junjie Fan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Jianpeng Cui: Investigation, Formal analysis. Tinglei He: Resources, Formal analysis. Tianhao Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Xinyu Yuan: Writing – review & editing. Jianwen Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Haotian Gao: Data curation. Hong Zhang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Chunling Li: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Songqing Hu: Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Shuangqing Sun: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the “National Natural Science Foundation of China” (52374060 and 51501226), “Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China” (ZR2024ME256), “Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province in China” (tsqn202211076), and “Research on Thermally Assisted Mixed-Phase Oil Recovery Technology” (2023ZG18).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2025.137935.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xie, H.B. Liu, Y. Li, J.Q. Tian, X.L. Qin, K.I. Shabani, C. Liao, X. Liu, Characterization of Pickering emulsions stabilized by OSA-modified sweet potato residue cellulose: Effect of degree of substitute and concentration, Food Hydrocolloids 108 (2020) 105915.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of intermediate wettability nanoparticles on oil-water emulsion stability
J. Pet. Sci. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Gavrielatos, R. Mohan, O. Shoham, Effect of intermediate wettability nanoparticles on oil-water emulsion stability, Journal of Petroleum Science and Engineering 152 (2017) 664-674.
